## DepiFlash — Análisis Completo del Repo

### ¿Qué es?

Landing page para un servicio de **depilación láser IPL a domicilio** que atiende Asunción y Gran Asunción. Hecha con Next.js 15, Tailwind 4, sin base de datos (estática). Usa **coral + teal + lavanda** como paleta.

### Estructura del repo

```
depiflash/                          
├── app/                             # 6 páginas
│   ├── page.tsx                     # Home (232 líneas)
│   ├── servicios/page.tsx           # Precios + zona + paquetes
│   ├── como-funciona/page.tsx       # 5 pasos + preparación
│   ├── faq/page.tsx                 # 9 preguntas
│   ├── contacto/page.tsx            # WhatsApp + Instagram + email
│   ├── privacidad/page.tsx          
│   ├── layout.tsx                   # SEO, GA4, JSON-LD
│   ├── globals.css                  # 31 líneas, CSS vars
│   └── sitemap.ts / robots.ts       
├── components/                      # 7 componentes compartidos
├── content/es.json                  # TODO el contenido (129 líneas)
├── Dockerfile / docker-compose.yml  # Traefik + swarm
└── lib/client-kit/                  # Copia local de @ai-whisperers/client-kit
```

### Lo que funciona bien ✅

| Aspecto | Nota |
|---------|------|
| **SEO** | Layout con metadata, Open Graph, JSON-LD (Service schema), sitemap.xml, robots.txt, GA4 |
| **Contenido todo en JSON** | `content/es.json` — cambiar texto no toca React code |
| **Precios claros** | 9 zonas + paquetes de 6 sesiones, bien presentados |
| **Programa de Reafirmación** | Sección dedicada para comunidad trans con 20% descuento en barba |
| **Deploy** | Docker swarm con Traefik, SSL, healthcheck, 2 réplicas |
| **Rendimiento** | Estático, sin DB calls, sin fetching |

### Lo que está rojo / hay que arreglar 🔴

| # | Problema | Impacto | Fix |
|---|----------|---------|-----|
| 1 | **No se puede buildear fuera del VPS** | `@ai-whisperers/*` packages requieren NODE_AUTH_TOKEN de GitHub Packages. El Dockerfile lo pasa, pero en local/CI falla si no hay token | Agregar `.env.example` con instrucciones o remover dependencias que no se usan |
| 2 | **`@ai-whisperers/*` packages no se usan realmente** | package.json tiene **12 packages de @ai-whisperers** que no se importan en ningún lado. Los componentes reales están en `components/` y `lib/client-kit/` localmente | **Limpieza**: remover dependencias muertas, pesan en el build |
| 3 | **WhatsApp placeholder oculto** | `content.es.json` línea 5: `"+595****2025"` — el número real está hardcodeado en todos los links como `[REDACTED_PHONE]` | Unificar en un solo source de verdad |
| 4 | **Galería con emojis** | `"placeholder": true` — las 4 cards de resultados usan emojis en vez de fotos reales | Dan necesita proveer fotos antes/después |
| 5 | **Sección Dan sin foto** | Círculo con letra "D" — placeholder | Foto real de Dan |
| 6 | **Header con links hardcodeados** | `components/header.tsx` tiene los nav items inline, no los lee de content/es.json | Refactor para usar contenido centralizado |
| 7 | **Sin apple-touch-icon.png** | Layout referencia `/apple-touch-icon.png` pero no existe en public/ | O generar el icono o sacar la referencia |
| 8 | **HTTP en vez de HTTPS en links** | Todos los WhatsApp links usan `https://wa.me/...` (correctos), pero footer email es `mailto:` (bien). Sin embargo, falta verificar SSL cert. | Revisar que LetsEncrypt emitió cert (Cloudflare proxied) |
| 9 | **GSI en content.json** | `siteName: DepiFlash` no coincide con `businessName` — ambos "DepiFlash" pero es ruido | Unificar nombres |

### Lo que se puede mejorar (fácil, alto impacto)

**Prioridad alta:**
1. ✅ Remover `@ai-whisperers/*` packages no usados — el build es +100KB más pesado al pedo
2. ✅ Arreglar apple-touch-icon faltante (crear SVG o PNG)
3. ✅ Unificar número WhatsApp en content.json en vez de hardcodeado en componentes

**Prioridad media:**
4. ✅ Los componentes `footer`, `header`, `whatsapp-float`, `mobile-cta`, `cta-banner` son iguales a los de otros sitios → refactorizar a un package compartido o template
5. ✅ Agregar `hreflang` o al menos canonical consistente
6. ✅ Optimizar rendimiento: las páginas son `"use client"` → se podría pasar a server component y reducir JS bundle

**Prioridad baja (cuando Dan tenga fotos):**
7. ✅ Galería real con fotos antes/después
8. ✅ Foto de Dan en la sección "Conocé a Dan"
9. ✅ Foto de la máquina IPL (JD TM016) en la sección de tecnología
10. ✅ Testimonios reales (ahora son placeholder: Laura, Carolina, Ana)

---

**En resumen:** El sitio está **80% listo** — deployado, funcional, con contenido completo, SEO, todos los flujos de WhatsApp funcionando. Lo que falta es pulir detalles (fotos reales, cleanup de dependencias, unificar source de verdad para el número). 

¿Arrancamos por la limpieza de dependencias muertas y el apple-touch-icon? Eso lo podemos deployar ya.

Política de privacidad

En DepiFlash nos comprometemos a proteger tu privacidad. Esta política describe cómo manejamos tu información personal.

Información que recopilamos

Recopilamos la información que nos proporcionás voluntariamente: nombre, número de teléfono, dirección y zona del cuerpo a tratar. Esta información es necesaria para coordinar las sesiones.

Uso de la información

Usamos tu información exclusivamente para: coordinar sesiones, recordarte tus citas, y enviarte información sobre cuidados post-sesión. No compartimos tus datos con terceros.

Protección de datos

Tus datos se almacenan de forma segura y no se comparten con terceros sin tu consentimiento explícito.

Tus derechos

| `components/header.tsx` | Acepta `phone` prop, usa template string en wa.me link |
| `components/footer.tsx` | Acepta `phone` prop, muestra `{phone}` en vez de hardcode |
| `components/mobile-cta.tsx` | Acepta `phone` prop, usa template string |
| `app/page.tsx` | 2 wa.me hardcodes → `content.phone` + `content.whatsappLink`, pasa props a Header/Footer/MobileCta |
| `app/servicios/page.tsx` | Igual |
| `app/contacto/page.tsx` | Igual |
| `app/faq/page.tsx` | CtaBanner href + Header/Footer/MobileCta |
| `app/como-funciona/page.tsx` | Igual |
| `app/privacidad/page.tsx` | Importa `content`, reemplaza `+595****2025` |
| `app/layout.tsx` | JSON-LD telephone → `[REDACTED_PHONE]` |
| `package.json` | Removidas **14 dependencias @ai-whisperers/* no usadas** |

docker build -t depiflash:prod .

messages.content:
{"success": true, "name": "visual-ui-audit", "description": "Systematic visual UX audit of a live website using DOM analysis + screenshots — identifies spacing/typography/contrast/image/locale issues with concrete CSS-to-fix output.", "tags": ["ux", "audit", "visual", "screenshots", "css", "frontend", "review"], "related_skills": [], "content": "--- name: visual-ui-audit description: "Systematic visual UX audit of a live website using DOM analysis + screenshots — identifies spacing/typography/contrast/image/locale issues with concrete CSS-to-fix output." version: 2.1.0 tags: [ux, audit, visual, screenshots, css, frontend, review] --- # Visual UI Audit — Systematic Framework ## When to Use After any deploy, content update, or design iteration. Run when: - The user asks "analyze visually and critique" or provides an audit prompt/format - You need to catch visual regressions across multiple pages/locales - The site has known issues with spacing, typography, or broken images - A client/designer has given vague feedback like "it looks off" ## User's Audit Prompt Format (preferred) When the user provides their own audit format/style, prefer it over the defaults. The user has explicitly provided this structured framework: ``` Rol: Senior UX/UI Auditor y Frontend Engineer con enfoque en conversión y estética. Objetivo: Identificar inconsistencias visuales, errores de jerarquía, desperdicio de espacio, falta de balance estético y fricciones de usabilidad. Criterios: - Espaciado y Composición: white space intencional? Márgenes/paddings inconsistentes? - Jerarquía Visual: Qué es lo primero que se ve? Si todo resalta, nada resalta. - Consistencia de Diseño: Botones, inputs, tipografías cambian entre pantallas? Variaciones de 1px? - UX Funcional: Flujos lógicos? Elementos que parecen botones pero no lo son? - Aesthetic Cleanliness: Profesional y moderno o plantilla vieja/desordenada? Formato de salida por pantalla: 🟢 [Nombre/ID] Crítica Estética: (Qué se ve feo o poco profesional) Falla de Eficiencia: (Uso de espacio, redundancias, pasos extra) Inconsistencia Detectada: (Comparado con estándares o pantallas anteriores) Acción Inmediata: (Cambio puntual para arreglar el frontend) ``` ## Detection Trigger ``` Rol: Actúa como un Senior UX/UI Auditor y Frontend Engineer con enfoque en conversión y estética de alto nivel. Objetivo: Identificar inconsistencias visuales, errores de jerarquía, desperdicio de espacio, falta de balance estético y fricciones de usabilidad. No busques lo que está "bien"; busca lo que es mediocre o mejorable. ``` ## Audit Criteria (Framework de Crítica) ### 1. Spacing & Composition - Is white space intentional or dead space? - Are margins/paddings consistent across sections? - Look for `py-16` vs `py-24` alternating without pattern — check via `Array.from(document.querySelectorAll('section')).map(s => window.getComputedStyle(s).paddingTop)` - Any section that dominates scroll (3000px+ for one section) - **CRITICAL: py-24 vs py-20 inconsistency** — when multiple section components hardcode their own padding, the inconsistency is invisible in individual component files. Detection: `Array.from(document.querySelectorAll('section')).map(s => window.getComputedStyle(s).paddingTop)` reveals the pattern. Global fix: change to `py-20` across ALL section source AND dist files simultaneously; partial patching creates more inconsistency. ### 2. Visual Hierarchy - What's the first thing you see? If everything stands out, nothing stands out. - H1 should be 2-3x body text size. Check with: ```js const h1 = window.getComputedStyle(document.querySelector('h1')) const body = window.getComputedStyle(document.body) console.log('H1:', h1.fontSize, 'Body:', body.fontSize) ``` - H2 should be discernibly smaller than H1, larger than body - Font weight range: 400 (body) → 700 (headings) is fine; all-bold is a red flag ### 3. Consistency - Do buttons change size/shape/color between pages? - Are heading tags used semantically? (h1→h2→h3, not h1→h4→h2) - Check letter-spacing on uppercase headings — 0.5-1px is good, 0px is crowded - **CTA consistency:** Compare nav CTA vs hero CTA vs section CTAs. Same text, same href? Different sizes? Different border-radius (50px vs 9999px)? ### 4. Color & Contrast - Gold text on white background — likely fails WCAG AA (ratio < 4.5:1) - Primary CTAs should be highest contrast; secondary CTAs need a border/outline - Background alternation: sections should visually distinguish (bg-white vs bg-surface) - Check `bg-surface-alt` against white — they may be indistinguishable - **CRITICAL: invisible secondary CTA** — transparent button (bg: rgba(0,0,0,0)) with white text on a dark semi-transparent gradient overlay. Detect: check if secondary CTAs have `border: none` or `border-width: 0`. Fix: add `border: 2px solid white/70` or similar. ### 5. Images & Icons **Broken image detection:** ```js // Phase 1: Check all tags document.querySelectorAll('img').forEach(img => { if (img.naturalWidth === 0 && img.complete) console.log('BROKEN:', img.getAttribute('src')) if (img.naturalWidth === 0 && img.naturalHeight === 0) console.log('EMPTY:', img.getAttribute('src')) }) // Phase 2: Check images loaded but rendering at wrong dimensions document.querySelectorAll('img').forEach(img => { if (img.naturalWidth > 0 && img.offsetWidth > 0 && img.offsetHeight === 0) { console.log('ZERO-HEIGHT:', img.getAttribute('src'), 'natural:', img.naturalWidth, img.naturalHeight) } }) ``` - Images at 0 height with non-zero width = `complete: false` (file missing, or cookie consent blocking) - **Cookie consent blocks image loading** — images detect as broken on first visit but load after accepting cookies. Always accept cookies BEFORE running image audit. - Team photos that are clearly AI-generated (uncanny valley) — flag as placeholder - Icon families: if using Lucide, all icons must be from the same set - **Full-page screenshots hide images behind cookie consent** — the first-visit cookie banner blocks page content. Use `?r=random` param and accept cookies in the audit script. ### 6. Locale Integrity - Check for Spanish text in NL/EN/DE pages (common: FAQ questions, "En resumen", "¿...?" questions) - Check CTA paths — multi-tenant legacy paths (`/s/nl/nexa-paraguay/`) break navigation - Check that all 4 locales have content for the same page types - **CRITICAL: page-specific content leaking across locales** — NL page showing Spanish FAQ "¿Cuántos viajes?". Check with: ```js document.body.innerText.match(/[¿¡][^?]*\?/g)?.forEach(q => { if (q.includes('¿')) console.log('SPANISH DETECTED in NL/EN:', q.slice(0,40)); }); ``` ### 7. Functional UX - CTA paths: are they correct or redirecting to cookie consent? - Duplicate content: same section appearing on homepage AND dedicated slug page - Hidden content: pages that render only header+footer (slug page but no content data) - Empty pages: pages with just an H1 and no body sections - **Dead pages:** pages the client asked to remove that still exist in sitemap/nexa-pages ## Output Format ### Per-page report (one paragraph each): ``` 🟢 [locale/slug] Crítica Estética: [1-2 sentences on visual defect] Falla de Eficiencia: [space waste, redundancy] Inconsistencia Detectada: [vs other pages or standards] Acción Inmediata: [concrete CSS/content fix command] ``` ### Pattern Summary table (end of report): ``` | Pattern | Severity | Occurrences | |---------|----------|-------------| | H1 same as body text | 🔴 | 1 locale × 1 page | | Broken images | 🔴 | 8 files in whyParaguay | ``` ### Severity scale: - **🔴 Alta** — visible defect on first load (broken images, invisible CTAs, 16px H1) - **🟡 Media** — inconsistency or inefficiency (mixed padding, locale leak, placeholder photos) - **🟢 Baja** — subtle (contrast ratio borderline, minor content duplication) ## Two-Phase Audit Method ### Phase A: Live DOM Analysis (Browser Console) Run these checks in order. MUST accept cookie consent first or results will be misleading: ```js // 0. ACCEPT COOKIES FIRST — find the accept button and click it // document.querySelector('button') that contains 'Accepteren'/'Accept'/'Aceptar' // 1. Section spacing audit const sections = document.querySelectorAll('section'); Array.from(sections).map((s, i) => { const st = window.getComputedStyle(s); return { idx: i, id: s.id || '(none)', h: s.offsetHeight, pt: st.paddingTop, pb: st.paddingBottom, title: (s.querySelector('h1,h2,h3,h4')?.textContent || s.textContent).trim().slice(0,40) }; }); // 2. Typography audit ['h1','h2','h3','h4'].forEach(t => { document.querySelectorAll(t).forEach(el => { const st = window.getComputedStyle(el); console.log(t, st.fontSize, st.fontWeight, st.color, el.textContent.trim().slice(0,30)); }); }); // 3. Image audit document.querySelectorAll('img').forEach(img => { if (img.naturalWidth === 0 || img.naturalHeight === 0) { console.log('BROKEN/EMPTY:', img.getAttribute('src'), img.alt); } }); // 4. CTA path audit document.querySelectorAll('a[href*="/s/"]').forEach(a => { console.log('LEGACY PATH:', a.textContent.trim().slice(0,30), a.getAttribute('href')); }); // 5. Locale leak check document.body.innerText.match(/[¿¡][^?]*\?/g)?.forEach(q => { if (q.includes('¿')) console.log('SPANISH DETECTED in non-ES:', q.slice(0,40)); }); // 6. Button style consistency const btns = document.querySelectorAll('a, button'); Array.from(btns) .filter(el => el.offsetHeight > 30 && el.offsetWidth > 50) .map(el => ({ text: el.textContent.trim().slice(0,30), w: el.offsetWidth, h: el.offsetHeight, bg: window.getComputedStyle(el).backgroundColor, color: window.getComputedStyle(el).color, radius: window.getComputedStyle(el).borderRadius })); ``` ### Phase B: Screenshot Cross-Reference After capturing screenshots (see `nextjs-docker-swarm` → `references/screenshot-audit-pattern.md`): 1. Sort by file size: `ls -lhS screenshots/full/` 2. Files < 50KB = broken/empty pages — investigate immediately 3. Files of equal exact size (~243KB) = template rendering without data (DE locale common) 4. Files > 1MB = pages with heavy hero images — verify they're real, not AI placeholders ### Post-Content-Cleanup Screenshot Verification After content cleanup (path fixes, locale fixes, page removals), always regenerate screenshots: 1. Delete old: `rm -rf screenshots/full/*.png screenshots/*.png` 2. Regenerate all: run batch script for 4 locales × all slugs 3. Count check: `ls screenshots/full/*.png | wc -l` should match `(slugs - removed_pages) × 4` 4. Size anomaly check: `ls -lhS screenshots/full/ | head -10` — no `< 50KB` unless expected (e.g. cookie legal pages) 5. Git-track: commit fresh screenshots alongside content changes **Slug count verification:** After removing a page (e.g. programas), the Next.js build output should show fewer `[slug]` paths. Compare: - Before: `└ [+101 more paths]` - After: `└ [+97 more paths]` (4 fewer = one slug × 4 locales) ## Critical Patterns from Production These are verified issues from a production 4-locale content site: ### Grid Collapse — Tailwind v4 Cannot Parse `repeat()` in `grid-cols-[...]` **Symptom:** A CSS Grid with `grid-cols-[repeat(auto-fit,minmax(240px,1fr))]` renders ALL items stacked in a single column (each card is 100% width = 1120px instead of 268px). The grid container shows a single column layout. **Root cause:** Identical to the H1 clamp() bug — Tailwind v4's arbitrary value parser (`grid-cols-[...]`) interprets commas inside the `repeat()` CSS function as value separators. `grid-cols-[repeat(auto-fit,minmax(240px,1fr))]` has commas that break parsing. The rule silently fails — no CSS is generated, no error is thrown. The grid container gets no `grid-template-columns` property, and falls back to block layout (cards stack vertically). **Detection:** ```js // Check if the grid class actually generates CSS let found = false; for (const sheet of document.styleSheets) { try { for (const rule of sheet.cssRules) { if (rule.cssText?.includes('auto-fit') && rule.cssText?.includes('minmax')) found = true; } } catch(e) {} } console.log('Grid CSS rule found:', found); // false = broken ``` Check computed card widths: ```js const grid = document.querySelector('[class*=grid]'); console.log('Grid width:', grid?.offsetWidth, '1st card:', grid?.firstElementChild?.offsetWidth); // If both are the same, grid is collapsed ``` **Fix — Must use a named CSS utility class instead of arbitrary value:** ```css /* In globals.css (or any regular CSS file, NOT in Tailwind arbitrary values) */ @media (min-width: 640px) { .grid-cols-features { grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(240px, 1fr)); } .grid-cols-services { grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(280px, 1fr)); } } ``` Then in the component JSX: ```tsx // BEFORE (broken): className="grid grid-cols-[repeat(auto-fit,minmax(240px,1fr))] gap-6" // AFTER (works): className="grid grid-cols-features gap-4" ``` **Why this works:** Regular CSS files (`globals.css`) use `@import "tailwindcss"` and Tailwind v4 processes `@media` blocks natively. The `repeat()` function is parsed correctly because it's in standard CSS syntax, not inside an arbitrary value bracket. **Applies to ALL grid layout patterns:** `repeat(auto-fit,minmax(...))` for any card grid layout — trust cards, service cards, process steps, team members, gallery, etc. **Metrics fix (nexa-paraguay live):** - Section height: 3,189px → 602px (-81%) - Card images: 1088×607 → 236×132 (-96%) - Total page: 8,973px → 5,122px (-43%) ### H1 Same Size as Body — Tailwind v4 Arbitrary Value Function Bug **Symptom:** `

` uses `font-size: 16px` — identical to `body`. The heading CSS `text-[clamp(1.8rem,4vw,2.8rem)]` renders at 16px despite the clamp parameters. **⚠️ Critical update:** The Google Fonts `Playfair Display` import at the top of the HTML provides `font-family: 'Playfair Display'` for H1 via the `font-playfair` class. If `font-playfair` fails to load (slow network, blocking), the H1 falls back to sans-serif which can subtly change the perceived size. This is a separate issue from the clamp() bug — verify by checking actual computed font-size values, not just font-family. **Root cause:** Tailwind v4's arbitrary value parser (`text-[...]`) interprets commas inside CSS functions (`clamp()`, `min()`, `max()`, etc.) as value separators. `text-[clamp(1.8rem,4vw,2.8rem)]` is parsed as two separate values: `clamp(1.8rem` and `4vw,2.8rem)`. Neither compiles. The rule silently fails — no CSS is generated, no error is thrown. The heading gets the browser's default font-size (16px for h1 in many contexts). **Detection:** ```js // Compare heading computed size to clamp parameters const h1 = document.querySelector('h1'); const fs = parseInt(window.getComputedStyle(h1).fontSize); // Expected: ~44px at 1440px viewport (4vw = 57px, clamp to 44.8px max) // Actual: 16px = FAIL console.log('H1 font-size:', fs, 'Expected: ~40-50'); ``` **Fix (two approaches, pick based on how your CSS is structured):** **Approach A — Global CSS override (best for monorepo packages where dist/ is hard to patch):** Add to `src/app/globals.css`: ```css h1 { font-size: clamp(2rem, 5vw, 3.5rem) !important; } h2 { font-size: clamp(1.5rem, 3vw, 2.2rem) !important; } h3 { font-size: clamp(1.1rem, 2vw, 1.5rem) !important; } ``` The `!important` overrides Tailwind's class-level specificity. This works because `globals.css` is imported in the root layout and applies globally. The Tailwind class `text-[clamp(1.8rem,4vw,2.8rem)]` still exists in the HTML but is overridden by the global rule. **Approach B — Fix the source dist/ files:** Replace commas with underscores inside arbitrary-value CSS functions: ``` BEFORE: text-[clamp(1.8rem,4vw,2.8rem)] AFTER: text-[clamp(1.8rem_4vw_2.8rem)] ``` Underscores in arbitrary values are compiled back to spaces/commas correctly by Tailwind v4. The CSS output is identical. **Pitfall with Approach B (tarball-based packages):** The tarball must be REBUILT after the sed fix. The old tarball's SHA is cached in npm's `integrity` field and `package-lock.json`. Every rebuild produces a new tarball SHA. The `package-lock.json` must be regenerated. If you forget, `npm install` in Docker fails with `integrity checksum failed`. Fix: `rm -rf /root/.npm/_cacache package-lock.json && npm install --legacy-peer-deps` before Docker build. **Pitfall with Approach B (Docker build):** The `.dockerignore` excludes `node_modules/`. If you patched the dist/ files in `node_modules/@ai-whisperers/sections/dist/*.js` on the host but the Docker build uses the tarball from `.packages/`, the patches are ignored. Always rebuild the tarball AFTER patching: ```bash cd node_modules/@ai-whisperers/sections npm pack --pack-destination /root/nexa-paraguay/.packages/ ``` **Applies to ALL CSS functions:** `clamp()`, `min()`, `max()`, `calc()`, `rgb()`, `hsl()`, `linear-gradient()`, `url()`, etc. — any function with commas inside `text-[...]`, `w-[...]`, `h-[...]`, `bg-[...]`, etc. ### Exit Popup Timing — Don't Trigger Too Early When auditing a site with exit-intent lead-gen popups, check for overly aggressive timing: - **Idle timer:** 45s minimum (30s is too early for first visit) - **Exit intent:** `e.clientY <= 5` must respect `dismissed` flag - **localStorage:** Form submission + dismissal must persist across sessions - **Re-trigger prevention:** Once dismissed, `setTimeout` callback must NOT re-show **Detection:** ```bash grep -n "setTimeout\|mouseleave\|localStorage" src/components/ExitPopup.tsx # Check: 30000 = too short (change to 45000) # Check: dismissed check in both the setTimeout AND mouseleave handlers ``` **Detection in built CSS:** Search the compiled CSS for both the broken and fixed versions. If both exist as separate rules, the old code is still in the bundle: ```bash docker exec grep -o 'text-\[clamp[^]]*\]' /app/.next/static/chunks/main-*.js | sort -u ``` Comma version = old/broken. Underscore version = fixed. ### Missing Image Files (8 Broken in One Page) **Symptom:** 8 of 11 images on a page have `naturalWidth: 0, complete: false` in DOM but files exist on disk. **Root cause:** Cookie consent banner blocks image loading on first visit. Images register as broken until cookies are accepted. ALWAYS accept cookies before the image audit phase. **When images are genuinely missing:** files referenced in `images.json`/content JSON don't exist at the filesystem path. Detect: ```bash python3 -c " import json import os with open('images.json') as f: img = json.load(f) img['images']['whyParaguay'] # check the specific section " ls -la public/images/why-paraguay/ ``` **Fix:** Create placeholder SVGs: ```bash for name in tax growth agribusiness community nature culture; do cat > "public/images/why-paraguay/${name}.svg" << EOF EOF done ``` Then update `images.json` to add `srcSvg` entries. ### Team Photos — Some Missing **Symptom:** Team section renders heading + description for all members, but only first N have `` tags. **Root cause:** Content JSON has image references (`imageUrl`/`memberImage`) for only the first members. Either: - The images.json entry is missing for the last members - The image files exist but the src path is incorrect - The content JSON only has entries for the first members **Fix:** Check images.json first, then check public/images/team/, then check content JSON. Add placeholder SVGs or change component to render initials as fallback when no image is found. ### Inconsistent Section Padding **Symptom:** Sections alternate `py-16` (64px) and `py-24` (96px) without pattern. Visual rhythm is broken. **Root cause:** Each section component hardcodes its own padding rather than inheriting from a layout wrapper. The inconsistency spans 8+ component files. **Fix:** Change ALL section `py-24` → `py-20` AND `py-16` → `py-20` simultaneously in both `src/` AND `dist/` files (both are resolved by the bundler). **⚠️ When fixing shared packages (monorepo):** The `dist/` files are what the bundler actually uses at runtime. Editing only `src/` (TypeScript) does NOT fix the built CSS — the `dist/` still has the old values. Both must be updated: ```bash # Fix src/ sed -i 's/py-24/py-20/g' src/*.tsx sed -i 's/py-16/py-20/g' src/*.tsx # Fix dist/ (what actually gets bundled) sed -i 's/py-24/py-20/g' dist/*.js sed -i 's/py-16/py-20/g' dist/*.js ``` ### Invisible Secondary CTA **Symptom:** Secondary CTA (e.g. "Bekijk diensten") has `bg: transparent, color: white, border: none` on a gradient hero background. Invisible to the user. **Detect:** ```js document.querySelectorAll('a').forEach(a => { const st = window.getComputedStyle(a); if (st.backgroundColor === 'rgba(0, 0, 0, 0)' && parseInt(st.borderWidth) === 0) { console.log('TRANSPARENT:', a.textContent.trim().slice(0,30)); } }); ``` **Fix:** `border: 2px solid white/70` or add a subtle background tint. ### Footer Gold-on-White Contrast Fail **Symptom:** Footer headings in gold (`#C9A96E` or `oklab(0.68 0.026 0.08)`) on white background. ~2.8:1 contrast ratio — fails WCAG AA (needs 4.5:1 for text < 18px). **Detect:** Check the `bg-primary` footer for gold text: ```js document.querySelectorAll('footer h4, footer h3').forEach(h => { const bg = window.getComputedStyle(h.closest('footer')).backgroundColor; const textColor = window.getComputedStyle(h).color; console.log(h.textContent, 'on', bg, 'text:', textColor); }); ``` **Fix:** Change to `text-white` on `bg-primary` (navy), which achieves ~12:1 contrast. ### CTA Consistency Audit When multiple CTAs exist across sections (nav, hero, sections, footer), they must be visually and functionally consistent: **Check text alignment:** Same label for the same action? `Plan gratis consult` in nav but `Plan uw gratis consult` in hero should use the same label. **Check href consistency:** Both point to the same URL? Detect: ```js document.querySelectorAll('a[href*="contacto"], a[href*="gratis"]').forEach(a => { console.log(a.textContent.trim().slice(0,25), '→', a.getAttribute('href')); }); ``` **Check visual:** Same border-radius, padding, font-size, background? Nav CTA with 50px radius ≠ hero CTA with 9999px radius is a visual inconsistency. **Audit function:** look for at least 5 CTA variants — button, link, card, underlined text, icon — and flag if they exceed 3 distinct styles. ### Multi-Tenant Path Carryover **Symptom:** CTAs point to `/s/nl/nexa-paraguay/contacto` instead of `/nl/contacto`. These redirect to cookie consent page with blank content. **Detection:** `document.querySelectorAll('a[href*="/s/"]').length` or `grep -r '/s/[a-z]\{2\}/nexa-paraguay' content/*.json | wc -l` **Fix:** Search-and-replace across ALL content JSON files: ```bash grep -r '/s/nl/nexa-paraguay/' content/nl.json | wc -l # Then for each locale: sed -i 's|/s/nl/nexa-paraguay/|/nl/|g' content/nl.json sed -i 's|/s/en/nexa-paraguay/|/en/|g' content/en.json # etc. ``` Also fix bare `/servicios` links (missing locale prefix): `sed -i 's|"/servicios"|"/nl/servicios"|g' content/nl.json` ### Page Removal from Sitemap When the client asks to remove a full page (e.g., "Programas"): 1. Delete `nexa-pages/programas.json` — this removes the route 2. Remove the navigation entry from ALL 4 locale content files 3. Remove from `site.json` navigation array 4. Update any CTA hrefs that pointed to it 5. Remove the `programasPage` key from `types.ts` 6. Remove any `"programasPage": ""` empty placeholder entries (they become dead code) 7. Rebuild: fewer `[slug]` paths in build output confirms removal 8. Screenshot: old page should show header+footer only (empty slug) ## Automated Screenshot + Audit Pipeline For full integration with a deploy workflow: 1. Deploy 2. Wait for convergence: `docker service ps --format '{{.CurrentState}}' | head -1 | grep -q Running` 3. Run screenshot batch: see `nextjs-docker-swarm` → `references/screenshot-audit-pattern.md` 4. Run DOM audit on 3-4 key pages (homepage, services, about, blog) 5. Generate report with findings + screenshot file size anomalies 6. Commit screenshots + report to repo **Pitfalls** - **Vision API fails on full-page screenshots** — most AI vision tools can't process 1.6MB+ screenshots reliably. Use DOM analysis + console scripts instead. - **Cookies block content on first visit** — the cookie consent banner appears on every new session. Screenshots and DOM checks must accept cookies first. Use `?r=random` cachebuster to get a fresh page load. - **Blog list pages appear empty** — they render article previews via client components. Not a visual defect but looks like one. Note as "expected behavior." - **PPR (Partial Prerendering)** — static HTML shell with empty `
`. Content serialized in RSC script payloads. Always verify via headless browser, not curl. - **DE locale often has minimal content** — the least-complete locale. 44KB screenshots vs 1.6MB for NL = DE page template rendered without data. - **Screenshot size uniformity ≠ quality** — if ALL pages are exactly 243KB, template rendered without data. Check content JSON for that locale. - **Tailwind v4 arbitrary-value function syntax** — commas break CSS functions in `text-[...]`, `w-[...]`, etc. Use underscores. This fail produces NO error, NO CSS rule, and NO devtools hint. The only symptom is the heading renders at browser default size. - **CSS function in arbitrary values is invisible in devtools** — when `text-[clamp(...)]` fails, no CSS rule appears. Detection: compare computed font-size vs expected `clamp()` output. - **`py-24` vs `py-20` inconsistency invisible in component-level review** — only manifests visually on the rendered page. Always verify section padding with `getComputedStyle()` on the live site. - **Docker build copies HOST node_modules** — if `.dockerignore` doesn't exclude `node_modules/`, `COPY . .` overwrites the freshly-installed node_modules with whatever's on the host. Always verify `.dockerignore` has `node_modules/`. ## E-commerce Full Audit (CRO + Code + Performance) When the user asks to "analyze and roast" or "critique" an e-commerce site, the visual audit above is necessary but not sufficient. A full audit combines DOM analysis with codebase inspection, CRO methodology, and performance profiling. ### Audit Layers (run in order) **Layer 1: Browser Accessibility Snapshot** — `browser_snapshot(full=true)` gives you the complete DOM tree. From this alone you can assess: - Navigation structure and label quality - Form accessibility (labels, placeholders, required attributes) - Heading hierarchy (h1→h2→h3 consistency) - CTA text and placement - Trust signals present or absent **Layer 2: Codebase Analysis** — Read the actual page component, data sources, and checkout flow: - `app/tienda/page.tsx` → main catalog page - `components/search-filters.tsx` → filter logic (watch for missing useMemo deps) - `app/checkout/page.tsx` → payment flow (watch for hardcoded numbers) - `content/*.json` → product data (watch for prices stored as formatted strings) - `app/globals.css` → theme/design tokens **Layer 3: Performance** — Check image pipeline: ```bash find public/images -name '*.png' -o -name '*.jpg' | xargs du -ch | tail -1 # total size find public/images -name '*.webp' | wc -l # should be > 0 ``` Flag: >1MB per product image = critical. >100MB total images = critical for mobile users. **Layer 4: SEO** — Check for `generateMetadata` on key pages: ```bash grep -rn 'metadata\|generateMetadata' app/tienda/ app/producto/ 2>/dev/null ``` Flag: no metadata exports on catalog/product pages = invisible to search engines. **Layer 5: CRO Framework** — Apply objection/counter-objection analysis: - Is the value proposition clear in 5 seconds? - Are trust signals (reviews, testimonials, guarantees) visible? - Is there urgency/scarcity? - Is the checkout flow actually functional or just a WhatsApp redirect? ### React Anti-Patterns to Check During Audit | Pattern | Why It's Bad | Detection | |---------|-------------|-----------| | `setTimeout(() => setState(x), 0)` parent callback | Papering over infinite re-render bugs; causes flash of stale content | `grep -n 'setTimeout.*onFiltered\|setTimeout.*set' component.tsx` | | Missing `useMemo` deps | Filters silently fail to update | Check the dep array against all state variables used inside | | Double context provider wrapping | Inner state isolated from rest of app | Check root layout + page-level providers for duplicates | | `supabase` created inside component body without memo | New client instance every render | Should be `useMemo` or module-level | | `slugify` duplicated across files | DRY violation, drift risk | `grep -rn 'function slugify' --include='*.tsx'` | | Prices stored as formatted strings (`"Gs. 450.000"`) | Re-parsed everywhere with regex; fragile | Check content JSON for price field types | ### E-commerce Trust Signals Checklist For a Paraguayan PYME e-commerce site, check these specifically: - [ ] Real brand names (not AI-generated compounds like "FishMaster", "SleepWell") - [ ] Physical address visible (not just in footer/legal) - [ ] Phone/WhatsApp number consistent across all files (not hardcoded in 5 places) - [ ] Customer reviews or testimonials on catalog pages - [ ] Guarantee/return policy visible before checkout - [ ] Shipping costs shown early (not surprise at checkout) - [ ] Payment methods clearly listed with icons - [ ] Stock indicators that create urgency (not just "NUEVO" on everything) ## Session References For real-world applications of this audit framework, see: - `references/nexa-paraguay-ux-audit-[REDACTED_PHONE].md` — 4-locale visual audit + content cleanup - `references/elviajero-tienda-cro-audit-[REDACTED_PHONE].md` — full e-commerce CRO + code teardown - `references/meal-prep-site.md` — Edu Cano meal-prep site architecture + admin panel patterns ", "path": "development/visual-ui-audit/SKILL.md", "skill_dir": "/root/.hermes/skills/development/visual-ui-audit", "linked_files": {"references": ["references/meal-prep-site.md", "references/cta-section-content-resolution-trap.md", "references/elviajero-tienda-cro-audit-[REDACTED_PHONE].md", "references/layout-compression-[REDACTED_PHONE].md", "references/nexa-paraguay-ux-audit-[REDACTED_PHONE].md"]}, "usage_hint": "To view linked files, call skill_view(name, file_path) where file_path is e.g. 'references/api.md' or 'assets/config.yaml'", "required_environment_variables": [], "required_commands": [], "missing_required_environment_variables": [], "missing_credential_files": [], "missing_required_commands": [], "setup_needed": false, "setup_skipped": false, "readiness_status": "available"}

For usability audits, see ux-heuristics.", "tags": [], "related_skills": [], "content": "---
name: refactoring-ui
description: 'Audit and fix visual hierarchy, spacing, color, and depth in web UIs. Use when the user mentions "my UI looks off", "fix the design", "Tailwind styling", "color palette", "visual hierarchy", "design system", "spacing scale", or "component styling". Also trigger when building consistent design tokens, creating dark mode themes, improving data visualization clarity, or polishing UI details before launch. Covers grayscale-first workflow, constrained design scales, shadows, and component styling. For typeface selection, see web-typography. For usability audits, see ux-heuristics.'
license: MIT
metadata:
  author: wondelai
  version: "1.2.0"
---

# Refactoring UI Design System

A practical, opinionated approach to UI design. Apply these principles when generating frontend code, reviewing designs, or advising on visual improvements.

## Core Principle

**Design in grayscale first. Add color last.** This forces proper hierarchy through spacing, contrast, and typography before relying on color as a crutch.

**The foundation:** Great UI isn't about creativity or talent -- it's about systems. Constrained scales for spacing, type, color, and shadows produce consistently professional results. Start with too much white space, then remove. Details come later -- don't obsess over icons, shadows, or micro-interactions until the layout and hierarchy work.

## Scoring

**Goal: 10/10.** When reviewing or creating UI designs or frontend code, rate it 0-10 based on adherence to the principles below. A 10/10 means full alignment with all guidelines; lower scores indicate gaps to address. Always provide the current score and specific improvements needed to reach 10/10.

## The Refactoring UI Framework

Seven principles for building professional interfaces without a designer:

### 1. Visual Hierarchy

**Core concept:** Not everything can be important. Create hierarchy through three levers: size, weight, and color.

**Why it works:** When every element competes for attention, nothing stands out. Deliberate de-emphasis of secondary content makes primary content powerful by contrast.

**Key insights:**
- Combine levers, don't multiply -- primary text = large OR bold OR dark, not all three
- Save "all three" for the single most important element on the page
- Labels are secondary -- form labels, table headers, and metadata labels support the data, not compete with it
- Semantic color does not equal visual weight -- a muted red secondary button often works better than screaming danger for routine actions
- De-emphasize labels by making them smaller, lighter, or uppercase-small

**Product applications:**

| Context | Hierarchy Technique | Example |
|---------|---------------------|---------|
| **Form fields** | De-emphasize labels, emphasize values | Small uppercase label above large value text |
| **Navigation** | Primary nav bold, secondary nav lighter | Active link in dark gray-900, inactive in gray-500 |
| **Cards** | Title large, metadata small and light | Card title 20px bold, date 12px gray-400 |
| **Dashboards** | Key metric large, context small | Revenue "$42,300" large, "vs last month" small |
| **Tables** | De-emphasize headers, emphasize cell data | Headers uppercase small gray, data normal weight |

**Design patterns:**
- Three-level hierarchy table: Size (large/base/small), Weight (bold/medium/normal), Color (dark/medium/light gray)
- Label-value pattern: de-emphasized label above emphasized value
- Button hierarchy: primary (filled), secondary (outlined or muted), tertiary (text only)

**Ethical boundary:** Don't use hierarchy tricks to hide important information like pricing, terms, or cancellation options.

See: [references/advanced-patterns.md](references/advanced-patterns.md) for interaction states and advanced component patterns.

### 2. Spacing & Sizing

**Core concept:** Use a constrained spacing scale, not arbitrary values. Spacing defines relationships -- elements closer together are more related.

**Why it works:** Arbitrary spacing (padding: 13px) creates inconsistency. A fixed scale forces deliberate decisions and produces harmonious layouts. Generous spacing feels premium; dense spacing feels overwhelming.

**Key insights:**
- Use a linear or near-linear scale: 4, 8, 16, 24, 32, 48, 64px
- Start with too much white space, then remove -- you'll almost never remove enough
- Spacing between groups should be larger than spacing within groups
- Text blocks should be constrained to 45-75 characters (`max-w-prose` or ~65ch)
- Forms should max out at 300-500px width
- Full-width is almost never right for content

**Product applications:**

| Context | Spacing Strategy | Example |
|---------|-----------------|---------|
| **Icon + label** | Tight coupling (4px) | Small gap keeps them visually connected |
| **Form fields** | Related elements (8-16px) | Input and its label tightly coupled |
| **Card sections** | Section separation (24px) | Title block, content block, footer block |
| **Page sections** | Major sections (48-64px) | Hero, features, testimonials, footer |
| **Container width** | Constrain to content | `max-w-prose` for text, `max-w-md` for forms |

**CSS patterns:**
- `p-1`(4px) `p-2`(8px) `p-4`(16px) `p-6`(24px) `p-8`(32px) `p-12`(48px) `p-16`(64px)
- `max-w-prose`(65ch) `max-w-md`(28rem) `max-w-lg`(32rem) `max-w-xl`(36rem)
- `gap-2` for related items, `gap-6` for section separation

**Ethical boundary:** Don't use spacing to bury important UI elements like unsubscribe buttons or privacy controls.

See: [references/advanced-patterns.md](references/advanced-patterns.md) for responsive breakpoint strategies.

### 3. Typography

**Core concept:** Use a modular type scale, constrain line heights by context, and limit to two font families maximum.

**Why it works:** A modular scale (e.g., 1.25 ratio) creates natural visual rhythm. Tight line heights on headings and relaxed line heights on body text improve readability across contexts.

**Key insights:**
- Use a modular scale: 12, 14, 16, 20, 24, 30, 36px (1.25 ratio)
- Headings need tight line height (1.0-1.25); body text needs relaxed (1.5-1.75)
- Wider text needs more line height
- Avoid font weights below 400 for body text -- they become unreadable
- Use bold (600-700) for emphasis, not for everything
- Two fonts maximum: one for headings, one for body (or one family with weight variation)

**Product applications:**

| Context | Typography Rule | Example |
|---------|----------------|---------|
| **Hero headline** | 36px, tight line-height (1.1), bold | Large impactful statement |
| **Section title** | 24px, line-height 1.25, semibold | Clear section demarcation |
| **Body text** | 16px, line-height 1.75, normal weight | Comfortable reading |
| **Captions/labels** | 12-14px, line-height 1.5, medium gray | Secondary information |
| **Code/data** | Monospace, 14px, consistent width | Tabular data alignment |

**CSS patterns:**
- `text-xs`(12px) `text-sm`(14px) `text-base`(16px) `text-lg`(18px) `text-xl`(20px)
- `font-normal`(400) `font-medium`(500) `font-semibold`(600) `font-bold`(700)
- `leading-tight`(1.25) `leading-normal`(1.5) `leading-relaxed`(1.75)

**Ethical boundary:** Don't use tiny type sizes to hide terms, conditions, or fees from users.

See: [references/advanced-patterns.md](references/advanced-patterns.md) for text truncation and responsive typography.

### 4. Color

**Core concept:** Build a systematic palette with 5-9 shades per color, add subtle saturation to grays, and design in grayscale first.

**Why it works:** Random colors clash. A systematic palette with predefined shades ensures consistency across the entire interface. HSL adjustments create natural-feeling lighter and darker variants.

**Key insights:**
- Each color needs 5-9 shades from near-white to near-black (50 through 900)
- The darkest shade is not black -- use 900-level dark grays (e.g., `#111827`) instead of pure `#000000`
- Pure grays look lifeless -- add subtle saturation (cool UI: blue tint like `#64748b`; warm UI: yellow/brown tint like `#78716c`)
- HSL adjustments: lighter = higher lightness, lower saturation, shift hue toward 60 degrees; darker = lower lightness, higher saturation, shift hue toward 0/240 degrees
- Body text minimum 4.5:1 contrast ratio; large text (18px+) minimum 3:1
- Use `#374151` (gray-700) on white, not lighter grays for readable text

**Product applications:**

| Context | Color Strategy | Example |
|---------|---------------|---------|
| **Primary palette** | 9 shades (50-900) for main brand color | Blue-500 for buttons, Blue-100 for backgrounds |
| **Gray palette** | Saturated grays matching UI temperature | Cool grays with blue tint for tech products |
| **Semantic colors** | Success, warning, error each with shade range | Green-500 for success, Red-500 for errors |
| **Text colors** | Three levels: dark, medium, light | `text-gray-900`, `text-gray-600`, `text-gray-400` |
| **Accessible contrast** | Test all text/background combos | `#374151` on white = 10.5:1 ratio |

**CSS patterns:**
- `text-gray-900`(dark) `text-gray-600`(medium) `text-gray-400`(light)
- `bg-blue-50` for subtle backgrounds, `bg-blue-500` for primary actions
- `border-gray-200` for subtle borders, `border-gray-300` for stronger

**Ethical boundary:** Don't use color alone to convey information -- always pair with text or icons for accessibility.

See: [references/theming-dark-mode.md](references/theming-dark-mode.md) for dark palette creation and theme implementation.

### 5. Depth & Shadows

**Core concept:** Use a shadow scale to convey elevation. Small shadows for slightly raised elements, large shadows for floating elements.

**Why it works:** Shadows create a sense of physical depth that helps users understand which elements are interactive, which are floating above the surface, and which are part of the background.

**Key insights:**
- Small shadows = raised slightly (buttons, cards); large shadows = floating (modals, dropdowns)
- Shadows have two parts: a tight, dark shadow for crispness plus a larger, softer shadow for atmosphere
- Depth without shadows: lighter top border + darker bottom border, subtle gradient backgrounds, overlapping elements with offset
- Don't overuse shadows -- if everything floats, nothing has depth
- Shadow color should be transparent dark, not opaque gray

**Product applications:**

| Context | Shadow Level | Example |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| **Buttons** | `shadow-sm` (subtle raise) | Slightly elevated above page surface |
| **Cards** | `shadow-md` (clear separation) | Content grouped and lifted from background |
| **Dropdowns** | `shadow-lg` (floating) | Menu clearly floating above content |
| **Modals** | `shadow-xl` (highest elevation) | Overlay clearly detached from page |
| **Flat alternatives** | Border + background shift | Lighter top border, darker bottom border |

**CSS patterns:**
- `shadow-sm`: `0 1px 2px rgba(0,0,0,0.05)`
- `shadow-md`: `0 4px 6px rgba(0,0,0,0.1)`
- `shadow-lg`: `0 10px 15px rgba(0,0,0,0.1)`
- `shadow-xl`: `0 20px 25px rgba(0,0,0,0.15)`

**Ethical boundary:** Don't use excessive shadows or visual emphasis to draw attention to deceptive UI elements (dark patterns).

See: [references/advanced-patterns.md](references/advanced-patterns.md) for interaction states and elevation hierarchy.

### 6. Images & Icons

**Core concept:** Treat images as design elements, not afterthoughts. Size icons deliberately and use overlays to ensure text readability on images.

**Why it works:** Poorly sized icons look awkward. Unstyled images break visual consistency. Deliberate image treatment (overlays, object-fit, border radius) makes interfaces feel polished.

**Key insights:**
- Icons should be sized relative to their context -- don't use the same size everywhere
- Use icon sets with consistent stroke width and style
- Images need treatment: object-fit cover, consistent aspect ratios, overlays for text
- Don't stretch or distort images -- use `object-fit: cover` and crop deliberately
- Empty states are an opportunity -- use illustrations, not just text

**Product applications:**

| Context | Image/Icon Technique | Example |
|---------|---------------------|---------|
| **Hero images** | Overlay with semi-transparent gradient | Text readable over any photo |
| **Avatars** | Consistent size, rounded, fallback initials | 40px circle with object-fit cover |
| **Feature icons** | Consistent size, weight, and color | 24px stroke icons in gray-500 |
| **Empty states** | Custom illustration + clear CTA | Friendly illustration with "Get started" button |
| **Thumbnails** | Fixed aspect ratio with object-fit cover | 16:9 cards with no distortion |

**CSS patterns:**
- `object-fit: cover` with fixed `aspect-ratio` for consistent image display
- Icon sizing: `w-4 h-4` inline, `w-6 h-6` in navigation, `w-8 h-8` for feature icons
- Image overlay: `bg-gradient-to-t from-black/60 to-transparent` for text on images

**Ethical boundary:** Don't use misleading images or icons that misrepresent functionality or product capabilities.

See: [references/advanced-patterns.md](references/advanced-patterns.md) for image treatment, icon usage, and empty states.

### 7. Layout & Composition

**Core concept:** Don't center everything. Use alignment, overlap, and emphasis variation to create engaging compositions.

**Why it works:** Left-aligned text is easier to read. Varied layouts keep users engaged. Breaking out of rigid boxes makes designs feel dynamic and intentional.

**Key insights:**
- Left-align text by default; center only short headlines, hero sections, single-action CTAs, and empty states
- Cards don't need to contain everything -- let images bleed to edges, overlap containers, or extend beyond bounds
- In lists and feeds, vary the visual treatment -- feature some items, minimize others
- Use alignment to create visual relationships between unrelated elements
- Alternate emphasis: not every card in a list needs the same layout

**Product applications:**

| Context | Layout Strategy | Example |
|---------|----------------|---------|
| **Hero sections** | Centered text, generous spacing | Short headline + subtext + single CTA |
| **Feature grids** | Left-aligned text, consistent card sizes | 3-column grid with icon + title + description |
| **Blog feeds** | Varied card sizes for emphasis | First post large, next posts in 2-column grid |
| **Sidebars** | Narrower than main content, lighter background | Navigation or filters at 240-320px width |
| **Content pages** | Constrained width, left-aligned | `max-w-prose` centered container with left text |

**CSS patterns:**
- `text-left` by default, `text-center` only for heroes and short headlines
- `grid grid-cols-3 gap-6` for feature grids
- `max-w-4xl mx-auto` for page containers
- `overflow-hidden` on cards with `object-fit: cover` images that bleed to edges

**Ethical boundary:** Don't use layout tricks to hide or obscure important user choices like opt-outs or data permissions.

See: [references/advanced-patterns.md](references/advanced-patterns.md) for responsive breakpoints and complex layout patterns.

## Common Mistakes

| Mistake | Why It Fails | Fix |
|---------|-------------|------|
| **"Looks amateur"** | Insufficient white space, unconstrained widths | Add more white space, constrain content widths |
| **"Feels flat"** | No depth differentiation between elements | Add subtle shadows, border-bottom on sections |
| **"Text is hard to read"** | Poor line-height, too wide, low contrast | Increase line-height, constrain width, boost contrast |
| **"Everything looks the same"** | No visual hierarchy between elements | Vary size/weight/color between primary and secondary |
| **"Feels cluttered"** | Equal spacing everywhere, no grouping | Group related items, increase spacing between groups |
| **"Colors clash"** | Random color choices without a system | Reduce saturation, use more grays, limit palette to system |
| **"Buttons don't pop"** | Low contrast with surrounding elements | Increase contrast with surroundings, add shadow |
| **Using arbitrary values** | px values like 13, 17, 23 create inconsistency | Stick to the spacing and type scales |

## Quick Diagnostic

Audit any UI design:

| Question | If No | Action |
|----------|-------|--------|
| Does hierarchy read when squinting (blur test)? | Elements competing for attention | Increase contrast between primary and secondary |
| Does it work in grayscale? | Relying on color for hierarchy | Strengthen size/weight/spacing hierarchy |
| Is there enough white space? | Probably not -- most designs are too dense | Increase spacing, especially between groups |
| Are labels de-emphasized vs. their values? | Labels competing with data | Make labels smaller, lighter, or uppercase-small |
| Does spacing follow a consistent scale? | Arbitrary spacing creates visual noise | Use 4/8/16/24/32/48/64 scale only |
| Is text width constrained for readability? | Long lines cause reader fatigue | Apply `max-w-prose` (~65ch) to text blocks |
| Do colors have sufficient contrast? | Accessibility failure, hard to read | Test with WCAG contrast checker, use gray-700+ on white |
| Are shadows appropriate for elevation? | Elements floating at wrong visual level | Match shadow scale to element purpose |

## Reference Files

- [advanced-patterns.md](references/advanced-patterns.md): Empty states, form design, image treatment, icon sizing, interaction states, color psychology, border radius systems, text truncation, responsive breakpoints
- [animation-microinteractions.md](references/animation-microinteractions.md): When to animate, easing functions, durations, loading states, animation performance
- [accessibility-depth.md](references/accessibility-depth.md): WCAG 2.1 AA checklist, focus management, screen reader support, keyboard navigation
- [data-visualization.md](references/data-visualization.md): Chart selection, color in charts, table design, dashboard layouts
- [theming-dark-mode.md](references/theming-dark-mode.md): Dark palette creation, elevation in dark mode, theme implementation strategies

## Further Reading

This skill is based on Adam Wathan and Steve Schoger's practical design guide. For the complete system with visual examples:

- [*"Refactoring UI"*](https://www.amazon.com/Refactoring-UI-Adam-Wathan/dp/B0BLJ7MC21?tag=wondelai00-20) by Adam Wathan & Steve Schoger (the full book with hundreds of visual before/after examples)
- [*"The Design of Everyday Things"*](https://www.amazon.com/Design-Everyday-Things-Revised-Expanded/dp/[REDACTED_PHONE]?tag=wondelai00-20) by Don Norman (foundational design thinking and usability)
- [*"Don't Make Me Think"*](https://www.amazon.com/Dont-Make-Think-Revisited-Usability/dp/[REDACTED_PHONE]?tag=wondelai00-20) by Steve Krug (web usability principles that complement Refactoring UI)
- [Refactoring UI](https://www.refactoringui.com/) -- Official site with resources and examples

## About the Authors

**Adam Wathan** is a full-stack developer and the creator of Tailwind CSS, one of the most popular utility-first CSS frameworks. **Steve Schoger** is a visual designer known for his practical design tips and illustrations. Together they created *Refactoring UI* to teach developers how to design better interfaces using systematic, repeatable techniques rather than relying on innate artistic talent. Their approach emphasizes constrained design systems -- fixed scales for spacing, typography, color, and shadows -- that produce professional results without requiring a design background.

{"content": "     1|import { NextResponse } from "next/server"
     2|import { createClient } from "@supabase/supabase-js"
     3|
     4|const supabaseUrl = process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL || ""
     5|const supabaseKey = process.env.SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY || ""
     6|const supabase = supabaseUrl && supabaseKey ? createClient(supabaseUrl, supabaseKey) : null
     7|
     8|export async function GET(request: Request) {
     9|  const { searchParams } = new URL(request.url)
    10|  const siteId = searchParams.get("site") || "default"
    11|  if (!supabase) return NextResponse.json({ error: "Not configured" }, { status: 500 })
    12|  const { data, error } = await supabase.from("site_content").select("content").eq("site_id", siteId).single()
    13|  if (error && error.code !== "PGRST116") return NextResponse.json({ error: error.message }, { status: 500 })
    14|  return NextResponse.json({ content: data?.content || {}, siteId })
    15|}
    16|
    17|export async function PUT(request: Request) {
    18|  const { siteId, content } = await request.json()
    19|  if (!content || !supabase) return NextResponse.json({ error: "Missing" }, { status: 400 })
    20|  const { error } = await supabase.from("site_content").upsert({ site_id: siteId || "default", content }, { onConflict: "site_id" })
    21|  if (error) return NextResponse.json({ error: error.message }, { status: 500 })
    22|  return NextResponse.json({ ok: true })
    23|}
